coredata.dc:title: A multi-step parameter identification of a physico-chemical lithium-ion battery model with electrochemical impedance data
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Wimarshana, Buddhi
Bin-Mat-Arishad, Izzuan
Fly, Ashley
coredata.dc:description: Physico-chemical battery models are widely used in the design and simulation of lithium-ion batteries due to their physically descriptive modelling approach. The model accuracy highly depends on the accurate identification of model parameters, yet accurate and feasible model parameterisation with state-of-the-art experimental techniques is laborious, expensive and tied to inherent measurement and estimation errors. Multi-step voltage-based data-driven parameter identification techniques are widely adopted in the literature to tackle this challenge. However, impedance-based parameter identification schemes lack a similar level of detailed analysis. Therefore, we propose a novel multi-step electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) based data-driven parameter identification framework to identify kinetic parameters of a physico-chemical battery model utilising particle swarm optimisation featuring metaheuristic optimisation capabilities. The parameter optimisation framework is designed methodically to identify parameter clusters with distinct sensitivities to specific EIS impedance regions, significantly improving identification accuracy. The generic particle swarm optimisation is fused with nature-inspired Darwinian events cross-generating new particles using selected parents to improve the algorithm’s predictions. The proposed data-driven parameter identification framework is benchmarked under multiple cell operating conditions and achieves a voltage prediction improvement of 28% for constant current discharge and 65% for transient duty cycles compared to an experimentally derived parameter set from the literature.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Sensitivity index analysis regions of EIS spectra. Analysed frequency bands are marked with H, M and L and, frequency limits separating each frequency region are marked with arrows.

Summary of the parameter sensitivities to EIS impedance frequency bands: H, M and L. Parameters are categorised into four groups according to sensitivities: H and M frequency bands (Charge-transfer region) sensitive, L frequency band (Warburg region) sensitive, sensitive to all-frequency bands (H, M and L), and, insensitive. The first bar (top most) in each parameter represent sensitivity to H frequency band, middle bar with M and bottom bar depicts the sensitivity of L band. Solid colour bars represent highest sensitivity due to real impedance and, hollow bars indicate imaginary impedances.
Flowchart of the multi-step, multi-SOC parameterisation framework.

Results of the numerical validation process for PSOg, MMPSOg and MMPSOd algorithms: (a) EIS model fitting comparisons, and (b) mean APEs of all algorithm-identified parameters grouped according to CT and Warburg sensitivities. The dash lines in each parameter group indicate the mean APEs of the respective group.

Experimental validation results: Parameterised P2D model performance with EIS simulations, Nyquist plots at (a) 100% SOC (b) 50% SOC (c) 20% SOC and, (d)–(f) depict Bode plots of imaginary components of (a)–(c) Nyquist, respectively. RMSE values of each simulation is given in (g).

Experimental validation results: Parameterised P2D model performance with voltage simulations, (a) 0.2C discharge with voltage relaxation (b) 1C pulse discharge with voltage relaxation, and (c) WLTP duty-cycle discharge. Below (a)–(c) show error between experimental and simulated voltage profiles. RMSE values for each test profile is given in (d) which also include segment-wise RMSE of CC segment and voltage relaxation segment of the 0.2C discharge test for better assessments.

Experimentally and data-driven parameterised model performance comparison. (a) Model simulation performances using experimentally identified and data-driven identified parameters compared with measured EIS at 100% SOC. (b) 0.2C discharge with voltage relaxation (c) 1C pulse discharge with voltage relaxation and, (d) WLTP discharge profiles of model simulated with experimentally and data-driven identified parameters are plotted with measured data. Error values are plotted below each plot (b)–(d).
A summary of the governing equations of the pseudo two-dimensional model [17,53]. Nomenclature is given in Table 2 and Section 5.
Summary of P2D model parameter ranges and, frequency bands with best parameter sensitivities. Fig. 2 illustrates the High (H), Medium (M) and Low (L) frequency bands.

head.ce:title: A multi-step parameter identification of a physico-chemical lithium-ion battery model with electrochemical impedance data

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Physico-chemical battery models are widely used in the design and simulation of lithium-ion batteries due to their physically descriptive modelling approach. The model accuracy highly depends on the accurate identification of model parameters, yet accurate and feasible model parameterisation with state-of-the-art experimental techniques is laborious, expensive and tied to inherent measurement and estimation errors. Multi-step voltage-based data-driven parameter identification techniques are widely adopted in the literature to tackle this challenge. However, impedance-based parameter identification schemes lack a similar level of detailed analysis. Therefore, we propose a novel multi-step electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) based data-driven parameter identification framework to identify kinetic parameters of a physico-chemical battery model utilising particle swarm optimisation featuring metaheuristic optimisation capabilities. The parameter optimisation framework is designed methodically to identify parameter clusters with distinct sensitivities to specific EIS impedance regions, significantly improving identification accuracy. The generic particle swarm optimisation is fused with nature-inspired Darwinian events cross-generating new particles using selected parents to improve the algorithm’s predictions. The proposed data-driven parameter identification framework is benchmarked under multiple cell operating conditions and achieves a voltage prediction improvement of 28% for constant current discharge and 65% for transient duty cycles compared to an experimentally derived parameter set from the literature.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The underlying data relating to this publication can be found at http://dx.doi.10.17028/rd.lboro.22598293.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) are being widely used in an increasing range of applications requiring energy storage, such as electric vehicles and smartphones. Nevertheless, the complex and coupled electro-chemo-mechanical internal interactions of the LIBs, often influenced by external operational conditions, have made it difficult to estimate and predict battery dynamics for safer-efficient control and better design decisions. Despite the challenges, battery researchers have put substantial efforts into emulating battery dynamics on all battery topology scales: from single cells to complete battery packs. Equivalent circuit battery models (ECMs), which are incorporated by many battery management systems (BMSs), can only simulate the voltage and temperature response to an input-current stimulus but fail to provide any insights into the internal operations of the cell [1]. In contrast, physico-chemical models (PCMs), although posing challenges to the hardware implementation with the current BMSs’ capacities, are capable of physical representation and supporting prognostics of the battery’s internal states [2–4], which are vital for advanced battery dynamics information and control.

It is envisioned that future generations of BMSs will require more sophisticated battery models with capabilities beyond the ECMs’ limited voltage and temperature estimations [1,5]. Whilst PCMs have gained increased interest towards PCM-based BMS [3], significant research can be identified focused on performing various battery tasks incorporating PCMs, such as internal state estimations and cell ageing identifications [6–13], which can be used for more effective and intelligent control by next-generation BMSs.

The core of many PCMs adopted by researchers are based on the Doyle–Fuller–Newman pseudo-2-dimensional (DFN P2D) model [14] and incorporate a large number of physics-descriptive parameters. Feasible and accurate identifications of all these PCMs’ parameters for a new cell pose a significant challenge [2] due to a large number of parameters with various sensitivities [15,16] and being mostly not directly measurable and inherent model complexities. Although parameter values being used among similar battery chemistries are observed [17] accurate model predictions are only warranted by an accurately parameterised model. Ecker et al. [18,19] for the first time introduced a complete parameterisation of a PCM using the geometric data and the parameters of the materials of a 7.5Ah high-energy Kokam cell, following invasive cell tear-down procedures inside an argon atmosphere. The authors also demonstrated a rigorous validation scheme of the parameterised model using charge–discharge tests at different C-rates and temperatures and using pulse profiles. Similarly, in [20,21], the same research group analysed a high-power 28Ah prismatic cell, which was intended for plug-in hybrid electric vehicles, and performed strenuous measurements on the opened cell. Both the electrochemical and thermal parameters were parameterised and validated experimentally. Cell tear-down and parameterisation on comparatively smaller-scaled cylindrical cells were seen in [22,23], where Chen et al. [22] measured cell parameters of a high energy 5Ah LGM50 21700 cylindrical cell, which is the same cell used in this study. O’Regan et al. [23] extended the analysis on the same LGM50 cell by including additional thermal parameters and parameter dependency on the state of charge (SOC) and temperature. The invasive model parameterisation frameworks presented by the works mentioned above describe thorough and necessary experimental procedures for measuring and estimating the essential parameters of particular cell chemistries and types. Nevertheless, those invasive cell parameterisation methods are inherently laborious, can be expensive and often, most parameter values are unique to individual cell designs, therefore, cannot be used to represent cell models with considerably different cell designs.

In contrast to invasive experimental cell parameterisation techniques, data-driven non-invasive methods are time and cost-effective. Further, if a confidentiality clause with the cell manufacturer prohibits cell opening for tear-down analysis, non-invasive cell parameterisation methods are the feasible alternative. On the other hand, data-driven model parameterisation techniques can be coupled with state-of-the-art battery remaining useful life (RUL) prediction schemes, such as [24,25]. However, inherent nonlinearities, time-scale separations in battery dynamics, intertwined physical phenomena and redundant definitions of the PCM pose a significant challenge in identifying model parameters [26]. In the literature, considering the data-driven non-invasive battery parameterisation research, various nonlinear optimisation algorithms have been employed to minimise the objective function, the sum of the squared error between experimental and model simulated data, to identify parameter values yielding an optimal fit for the target data. However, it was not until work by Santhanagopalan et al. [27] that there were no considerable efforts to estimate parameters using PCMs because of computational constraints [28]. Santhanagopalan et al. [27] used the Levenberg–Marquardt nonlinear regression to estimate transport and kinetic parameters from experimental charge/ discharge voltage curves measured at 35 °C under four constant-current rates. The authors identified five model parameters, including diffusion of lithium ions in the positive electrode and electrochemical reaction rate constants of the electrodes. Boovaragavan et al. [28] parameterised four kinetic and transport parameters of a reformulated PCM using the Jacobian-based Gauss–Newton method from the synthetic experimental data (virtual cell) obtained using computer-generated random numbers. Berliner et al. [29] explored the parameter space by applying the Markov chain Monte Carlo to identify the quantifiability of the P2D model parameters of a Lithium–Cobalt-Oxide (LCO)/Graphite cell. A reduced order PCM has been used in [30], and nine selected parameters were parameterised for synthetic dynamic driving data employing nonlinear least square regression with the Levenberg–Marquardt method. The study corroborated increased model accuracy when only a subset of better identifiable parameters was used for the parameterisation instead of considering all the parameters representing the model. In Jin et al. [31], proposed a computationally feasible two-step model parameterisation approach employing a nonlinear least-squares regression technique as the parameter optimiser. The authors performed parameterisation in two groups, where thermodynamic and kinetic parameter groups were estimated by fitting to smaller constant-current discharges and (designed) highly-dynamic pulse (dis)charges of a 12 Ah Nickel–Cobalt–Aluminium (NCA) pouch lithium-ion cell.

In the literature, more research has been observed in identifying the physical parameters of PCMs utilising more futuristic, nature-inspired and gradient-free metaheuristic algorithms transiting from more classical gradient and jacobian-based optimisation techniques. Although the jacobian-based methods might fall into local optima, particularly with the large number of parameters to be estimated [32], metaheuristic algorithms are immune to local minima traps due to underlying stochastic mechanisms [33]. Genetic algorithms (GAs) use random selection, mutation and crossover stochastic mechanisms to search the nonlinear solution space for optimal model parameters that give the best experimental data representation. Hu et al. [34] introduced GAs to data-driven battery parameterisation by optimising ECM parameters of both lithium-ion and NiMH chemistries and analysed model fitting to voltage response for an asymmetrical step current input profile. Extending the data-driven parameter identification to the PCM scale, which denotes considerably more difficulty with more model parameters compared to ECM, Forman et al. in [35] evaluated the identifiability of the complete P2D model parameter set using Fisher information and authors identified 88 parameters with GA using constant-current charge and discharge voltage data. It is worth noting that the inclusion of the insensitive parameters in the parameter identification step only adds computational burden without any significant performance improvement of the model [2]. Compared to GA, particle swarm optimisation (PSO) offers cognitive and social combined guidance strategy with memory capabilities of previous best solutions. The cognitive and social search components allow better exploration and exploitation of the search space avoiding local minima, and memory of previous states allow faster convergences. Therefore, PSO has been utilised in parameter identification studies at different complexities. Rahman et al. [36] introduced PSO to PCM parameter identification by identifying four model parameters, including solid phase diffusivities and kinetic reaction rate constants, and validated the model with only 1C voltage profiles of aged cells. In [37], Yu et al. identified parameters of a third-order ECM using PSO. Yang et al. [38] utilised the PSO algorithm to identify 16 electrochemical model parameters by parameterising the model parameters with 0.5 C discharge voltage profiles. A multi-step PSO-based parameterisation procedure was proposed by Chu et al. [39] to identify parameters of a lumped-parameter PCM. It is worth highlighting that the multi-step approach has helped to reduce the number of identified parameters at each step, making the identification process less difficult and more robust and accurate [39]. The trend of continuation of utilising nature-inspired metaheuristic-based parameter estimation techniques is seen with more state-of-the-art techniques in the more recent studies, such as [2,40–42]. Li et al. studied the identification of 20 PCM parameters utilising a systematic approach based on a two-step identification process, where the cuckoo search algorithm is coupled with the data-driven parameter estimation approach [2]. The two-step parameter identification framework displayed improved parameter identification for high- and medium-sensitive parameters to low and high-dynamic voltage profiles. Kim et al. [40] proposed using a novel technique in which a neural network cooperated with a genetic algorithm and identified eight PCM parameters. Despite the larger output voltage errors, authors claim the suitability of the genetic algorithm and neural network cooperative optimisation for recommending highly probable parameter candidates. The bald eagle search algorithm was utilised by Fathy et al. [41] to identify parameters of a simplified PCM. The same research group studied the white shark and whale optimisation strategy to identify parameters of the same battery model in [42]. These studies provide the state-of-the-art metaheuristic approaches utilised in the LIBs parameter identification process and highlight the accurate predictions over the more classical gradient and Jacobian-based optimisation techniques. In this study, we employ PSO due to its proven better exploration and exploitation of the search space capabilities [36,38,39,43] and further improve optimisation capabilities suitably for the case study by incorporating Darwinian events as discussed in Section 2.3.4.

Different voltage profiles covering low and high dynamics have been widely adopted among the data-driven LIB parameter identification studies as these are commonly accessible in BMS and easy to perform [44]. The dynamics of input signals used for sensitivity analysis and parameter estimation have a large effect on the outcome as this dictates the overall accurate parameter estimation [16,45] and, on the other hand, corresponds to the model’s intended usage [44]. For example, Zeng et al. [45] observed that constant current (CC) charging and discharging data are more suitable for the identification of capacity-related parameters whilst kinetic parameters are more sensitive to high-dynamic duty cycle profiles leading to improved identification. Most studies employ CC [46,47] or drive-cycle [2,31,43,48] voltage profiles as input signals to parameter estimation frameworks. Bi et al. utilised CC charge and discharge voltage profiles to estimate solid-phase diffusion coefficients of positive and negative electrodes and the film resistance of a reduced order model (ROM) representing the beginning of life (BOL) battery state. Nevertheless, the authors did not attempt to estimate some kinetically important parameters, such as electrode reaction rate coefficients which are important for representing dynamic operating conditions. Based on the electrochemical-thermal coupling model of LIB, Liu et al. performed parameter sensitivity analysis for CC voltage profiles of 23 model parameters, but the study did not extend to parameter identification [47]. On the other hand, many recent studies are observed utilising high dynamics in their parameter identification approaches targeting improved parameter identification. Real-world dynamics representative dynamic stress test (DST) data were utilised as parameter optimisation framework input data by Cai et al. [48]. The authors utilised the Fisher information matrix and Cramer–Rao bound for selecting the data-richest point for parameter identification. Despite the lower error percentages of the voltage simulations, the analysis is limited to a few model parameters of an ECM; therefore, the potential of the method with more complex PCMs needs to be explored. In [31], Jin et al. identified thermodynamic and kinetic parameter groups of a DFN-based battery model with constant-current and highly-dynamic pulse voltage profiles. Jin et al. highlighted the parameter estimation improvements based on a step-wise optimisation strategy in which thermodynamic and kinetic parameters were grouped and identified separately. Li et al. [2] incorporated low dynamic discharge voltage profiles and high-dynamic multi-pulse tests and standard duty-cycles in a train–test–validation scheme to identify 20 model parameters selected based on a parameter sensitivity analysis. Quasi-static and dynamic voltage measured data from a general Nickel-rich LIB at different life stages are utilised by Gu et al. [43] for estimating model parameters. Authors employed Sobol sensitivity-based parameter grouping to identify the parameters utilising a step-based optimisation technique. Interestingly, the results indicated about two-fold improved parameter identification compared to the single-shot parameter identification scenario. For more high-dynamics rich voltage-based parameter identification studies, the reader is referred to [44] where authors summarise the recent studies.

Despite performing well with better voltage fittings to similar model-fitted current profiles, parameters only identified through CC load profiles show poor model simulations in the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) domain [16], which stress the requirement of correct identification of parameter groups spread across several time constants with distinct dynamics. Although cell voltage has been the widely adopted measurement state for model parameterisation, EIS is a powerful transient analysis technique to study various battery electrochemical phenomena and has the potential in parameter identification yet is less explored its potential, which is the main scope of this paper. In [49], Murbach et al. created an open-source fitting tool for experimental EIS data with 38,800 simulations using a P2D model. This database can be used as a lookup table to find the best matches of parameter values combinations for a given data set. Nevertheless, the technique is less feasible as even slight changes in the cell chemistries or ageing of the cell may require a new set of simulations. In [50], electrode level model parameters were identified with high–medium–low frequency bands of the EIS, where negative electrode parameters were identified with high frequency data and, mid and low frequency data were utilised to identify positive electrode parameters. However, it is not clear how this approach was utilised in identifying certain parameters which have very low or no effectiveness in certain frequency bands, such as the negative electrode diffusivity parameterised with high frequency EIS data. Moreover, validation of the model performance with the identified parameters was not provided for a better assessment of the technique. A multi-step PSO-based parameterisation procedure was proposed by Chu et al. [39] to identify the parameters of a lumped-parameter PCM. Despite the incorporation of EIS measurements as part of the parameter identification framework, the analysis was limited for a lumped-parameter model, and the performance of such technique with a DFN model is yet unknown. Furthermore, EIS possesses the ability to identify parameters with distinct kinetic time-scales. For instance, electrode kinetic reactions are more accurately captured by the charge-transfer semi-circle region, while solid-phase diffusion coefficients exhibit greater sensitivity in the diffusion tail region of the EIS spectra [16]. However, this potential of EIS was not explored in the work presented by Chu et al. [39], as the authors primarily focused on voltage measurements as the primary target for parameterisation. Consequently, the primary objective of this paper is to address this gap in knowledge and investigate the capability of EIS for accurately identifying kinetic parameters of a DFN-based PCM.

Although thorough performance analysis and adequate validation are covered with the voltage data-driven parameter identification studies, such as in [2], such detailed analysis is not present with EIS spectra-driven parameter identification. Therefore, to identify the kinetic parameters of a PCM, for the first time, we propose a multi-step, multi-SOC parameter identification framework, which utilises distinct parameter sensitivities to impedance data at certain Nyquist frequency bands to gain better accuracy with the metaheuristic optimisation approach. An improved PSO algorithm optimises the impedance-derived cost function with evolution-inspired Darwinian events for batter accuracy in parameter estimation. Finally, the model performance with EIS-identified parameters is thoroughly assessed with numerical benchmarks and compared with an invasive experimentally measured parameter set from the literature.

The content of the paper is organised as follows: Section 2 introduces the methodology covering cell modelling details, parameter sensitivity analysis and multi-step, multi-SOC parameter identification framework with PSO algorithm. Section 3 covers the data-driven parameter identification results and performance assessments of the proposed framework with numerical benchmarks by comparing the model performance with data-driven and experimentally identified parameter sets. Finally, Section 4 concludes the important findings.

In this study, a commercially available cylindrical LG M50 21700 18.20 Wh (LGM50) pristine lithium-ion battery comprising an Nickel–Manganese–Cobalt-Oxide (NMC) 811 positive electrode and bi-component Graphite-SiOx negative electrode [22] was assessed to demonstrate the performance of proposing impedance-based data-driven non-invasive parameter identification of a P2D cell model. Although the P2D model based on the DFN modelling contains more than 20 physical parameters [51], not all these parameters render significant sensitivities towards the considered cell output’s dynamics. For example, in [16], for the same analysed cell in this study, authors observed that the electrodes’ kinetic reaction rates displayed significantly higher sensitivities for the mid-high frequencies (1–100 Hz) EIS spectra. However, kinetic reaction rates were insensitive to model voltage outputs for constant low-rate discharge currents with lesser dynamics. Therefore, in this study, as the first step, the model parameters were suitably selected with higher sensitivities to EIS impedance data and then optimised with the metaheuristic approach based on PSO for improved model accuracy. A multi-step, multi-SOC optimisation procedure was methodically utilised with PSO to minimise the impedance-derived cost function.

A P2D model based on the DFN model was used to simulate the physico-chemical behaviour of the LGM50 using the commercial software package COMSOL Multiphysics version 6.0. The battery module of the software package was utilised to develop a 1D model of the LGM50 cell. Fig. 1 illustrates schematics of the 1D model, which comprises three 1D sub-domains: the negative electrode, the separator and the positive electrode.

These sub-domains are spatially discretised into a set of nodes where partial differential equations are solved for the cell kinetics across the thickness of the electrodes
( x )
. At each node, a pseudo dimension
( r )
is used to model and solve for the solid particles of the electrode materials. Active material particles of both positive and negative electrodes are assumed to be spherical with specific radii and surrounded by the electrolyte where solid-to-electrolyte volume fractions of porous particles are given by

. Whilst electrode thicknesses are measured across the thickness of the jelly roll of the cell, surface area ( A ) is the electrode surface area of the unrolled jelly roll which is measured orthogonal to the electrode thicknesses. The 1D P2D model numerically solves for the current densities, which are scaled with electrode surface area ( A ) to derive the cell level values [53]. Table 1 summarises the governing equations and the boundary conditions of the P2D model. Detailed discussions of the P2D model derivation can be found in [53,54].

Custom mesh settings were used during modelling with the maximum mesh size set to

m for all three sub-domains. Electrode level open circuit voltage (OCV) data obtained from [22], where the same cell has been experimentally parameterised, were used in the model. The frequency domain perturbation study step was used to simulate the EIS spectra at particular SOC levels of interest during the optimisation. The numerical model used in this work is available for download through the underlying data link referenced in the acknowledgements.

DFN model-based PCMs contain more than 20 physics descriptive parameters [51]. Experimental invasive methodologies for identifying the PCM parameters are expensive, time-consuming and require higher expertise [18,19,22,23]. On the other hand, data-driven non-invasive parameterisation can be significantly improved with a prior parameter sensitivity analysis to help select the group of parameters that significantly affect the model output of interest [2,55]. In our previous study, parameter sensitivities of 30 physics descriptive parameters of the P2D COMSOL battery model for EIS spectra were studied with the One-Factor-At-Time (OFAT) framework [16], which allows the study of parameter sensitivity by one parameter at-a-time by varying only the parameter of interest within a range whilst keeping other parameters at their nominal values [56].

Table 2 tabulates the total 30 P2D model parameters, and parametric value ranges selected by referring to an extensive number of literature related to NMC/Graphite and NMC/Graphite-SiOx cell chemistries to provide realistic parameter values for the sensitivity analysis. The superscripts
+ , − , s
of the parameters represent the positive electrode, negative electrode and separator, respectively.

P2D model parameters were grouped into three groups: geometric, transport and kinetic and concentration. Although in our previous sensitivity study [16], the charge transfer coefficient α was considered a constant of 0.5, it was regarded as a tuned parameter in this work to better characterise the actual interfacial kinetic reaction by the Butler-Volmer relationship (Table 1) [23,74]. Although several experimental techniques for measuring ECSA are utilised in electrochemical research, such as Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) measurements, the correlation between BET measurements and ECSA is questioned in some recent literature [75]. The same technique of using a calibration factor with COMSOL P2D models to mimic specific cell characteristics has been seen used by others [52,55].

PCM parameters show different levels of sensitivity on EIS spectra. Significant variations in the EIS spectra, caused by the variations of the model parameter itself, indicate higher sensitivity of that particular parameter. In our previous parameter sensitivity study [16], each investigated parameter was simulated at uniformly distributed five discrete values (within realistic value boundaries based on a collection of literature) to assess the variation of EIS spectra at the five discrete parameter values. The parameters analysed for the sensitivity study, including the newly added parameters in this study ( α and
a
f
v
) and the parameter value ranges derived following literature are given in Table 2.

As depicted in Fig. 2, Nyquist spectra were segmented into three frequency bands, i.e. high (H), medium (M) and low (L), and parameter sensitivities at each frequency band were analysed to identify the parameters representing distinct time scale dynamics. Contrary to other sensitivity studies using raw EIS data, non-dimensional impedance data states derived from real and imaginary Nyquist data were proposed for the impedance-based parameter sensitivity analysis. The proposed technique demonstrated accurate sensitivity analysis with the ability to pick the truly affected frequency band(s) compared to the sensitivity analysis techniques based on the direct EIS impedance states [16]. Parameters affecting the overall ohmic resistance of the model, such as electrode conductivities, were observed to have higher sensitivities at the high-frequency band (H). Whereas charge-transfer kinetics parameters, like electrode reaction rate coefficients, depicted significant sensitivities in the mid-frequency (M), and diffusion coefficient parameters were more sensitive at the low-frequency band (L).

Fig. 3 illustrates both real and imaginary impedance sensitivities accordingly depending on whichever state, i.e. real or imaginary impedances, yield the highest sensitivity [16]. For example, the negative electrode’s Bruggeman coefficient showed the highest sensitivity index value for frequency band H with the sensitivities calculated on real impedance data, whilst the imaginary portion of the Nyquist data yielded the highest sensitivity for frequency band M. Out of the 30 analysed parameters, 27 were identified as impedance-sensitive based on the normalised sensitivity index over 0.05. Parameter sensitivity groups highlighted with different colours in Fig. 3 are explained in Section 2.3.1.

It is important to note that the LGM50 cell analysed for the parameter sensitivity is a high-energy cell. Therefore, the sensitivity groups summarised in Fig. 3 can considerably vary for a high-power cell. On the other hand, considering PCM parameterisation, distinct parameter sensitivities associated with EIS frequency bands can be utilised in a multi-step parameterisation framework for robust and more accurate model tuning.

Invasive experimental parameterisation of PCMs are expensive, time-consuming, require higher expertise and, importantly, higher accuracies and consistencies are not always guaranteed as most parameters, such as diffusion coefficients and double layer capacitances, are not directly measurable. Therefore in this work, we propose for the first time, a multi-step, multi-SOC parameterisation framework (described in Section 2.3.3) operating on EIS-based impedance data utilising metaheuristic optimisation (covered in Section 2.3.4).

This section presents a novel multi-step, multi-SOC parameterisation framework motivated by parameter sensitivity analysis combined with inter-step cross-validation. The aim of the multi-step, multi-SOC framework is to provide more accurate kinetic parameter identification (albeit at the cost of increased computational time) by utilising distinct parameter sensitivities to certain EIS spectra regions and, considering SOC dependency of kinetic parameters to achieve accurate model representation across full operational voltage range of LIB.

For a more straightforward approach with reduced computational overhead, two distinct impedance regions were selected considering the sensitivities of the model parameters to EIS Nyquist outputs, i.e. the charge transfer (CT) region corresponding to impedance frequency bands H and M in Fig. 2 and the Warburg impedance region (Warburg for short) corresponding to impedance frequency band L in Fig. 2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Tuning parameters for the parameterisation framework
ce:section.ce:para: Among the initial 27 impedance-sensitive parameters, 15 parameters were selected and, considered for the parameterisation framework. The chosen parameters are highlighted in Table 3. Selecting a lesser number of effective parameters helps improve the accuracy of the predictions of the multi-step metaheuristic-based parameterisation framework as it reduces the possible solution space for the complex nonlinear problem.

Considering the geometric parameters, electrode particle radius,
r
p
+
and
r
p
−
, were chosen for the parameterisation process due to the spread of particle sizes and shapes of the active materials leading to considerable uncertainty in providing a single representative value [22]. The rest of the geometric parameters, i.e. electrode thickness, L , electrode surface area, A , and electrode active material volume fraction,
ϵ
s
, were not included in the optimisation process and kept fixed with the values from [22]. This study focuses on the parameterisation of a PCM for a pristine cell which alleviates the possible deviations in the cell parameters caused by cell ageing. Therefore, it was fair to assume that LGM50 cell measurements performed by Chen et al. [22] on above mentioned fixed geometric parameters could be directly considered in this study with higher confidence due to the relatively higher measurement accuracies of the respective parameters and direct measurability of most of them. The same parameterisation-exclusion assumptions were made on several transport parameters, including electrode and electrolyte conductivities, σ and
κ
e
, respectively, and electrolyte diffusivity,
D
l
e
, and three of the kinetic group parameters, i.e. electrode maximum ionic concentration,
c
s
+
and
c
s
−
, and initial electrolyte concentration,
c
e , 0
. During the parameterisation process, these excluded parameters were kept constant at the mid-points of the parameter boundaries summarised in Table 2.

This section describes the multi-step, multi-SOC framework proposed in this study for model parameterisation solely based on impedance data. The proposing framework is built upon the distinctive parameter sensitivities for CT and Warburg regions, and the SOC dependency of the parameters. Moreover, the framework acts as an inter-step cross-validator during the parameter optimisation process, which helps mitigate the overfitting of data at certain Nyquist regions.

Parameters influence the model output in two ways: first, as already discussed earlier, the sensitivity of a certain parameter within a certain frequency band(s) of the Nyquist spectra, and second, the dependency of the parameter on the SOC of the cell [44].

Table 4 tabulates the finalised parameter clusters with, similar trends in parameter sensitivity in CT and Warburg regions, and considering SOC dependency. The parameter clusters are named A, B, C and D for facile referencing of parameters included in each cluster throughout the paper.

Based on the sensitivity and SOC-dependency features of the parameter clusters summarised in Table 4, the parameterisation framework was designed with multi inter-step optimisation stages comprising of multi-SOC intra-sub-step parameterisation phases. The parameterisation framework is built with four main consecutively-run steps, primarily focusing on tuning the parameters using their best sensitivity conditions. Each step consists of three sub-steps assigned to run at three different SOC levels: 100%, 50% and 20%. This multi-SOC-based parameterisation is contrary to the other single-SOC-based parameterisation studies and is intended to improve model performance across a wider cell SOC range. Although more SOC sub-steps during the parameterisation would increase the model accuracy across the SOC range, that would also increase the computational times. The proposing multifold parameterisation procedure is depicted as a flowchart in Fig. 4.

As shown in Fig. 4, the parameterisation framework initiates from step 1 and progresses through consecutive steps whilst performing sub-steps. As outlined below, the parameterisation framework is mainly structured around the two impedance sensitivity regions, CT and Warburg.

ce:list-item.ce:para: step 1: This step fits the CT-sensitive parameter clusters A, B and C with the CT region of the EIS target data, whilst cluster D is fixed at the values given by Chen et al. [22]. The step comprises three sub-steps where similar tuning strategies are deployed with the three SOC settings, 100%, 50% and 20%. Considering the SOC levels, 50% SOC is performed as the first sub-step as it is the middle value. Then, whilst keeping the 50% tuned cluster A fixed, clusters B and C are optimised with the 100% and 20% SOC levels consecutively.

step 2: Although this step targets fitting cluster C parameters sensitive to both CT and Warburg impedance regions, cluster D was also included considering the higher sensitivity indices shown by the cluster in the Warburg region. Clusters A and B are kept fixed at their step 1 results. PSO algorithm (refer Section 2.3.4) use tuned cluster C values from step 1 as the initial values for the respective parameters during the optimisation. Same as step 1, this step also proceeds with three consecutive sub-steps related to 100%, 50% and 20% SOCs.

step 3: This step is similar to step 1 and performs re-tuning of the CT-sensitive parameters to achieve final tuned values for clusters A, B and C. While performing parameter tuning, cluster C parameterisation boundaries are narrowed to 0.8–1.2 times the results found in step 2 for cluster C parameters. These narrow boundaries are included to avoid considerable changes in the Warburg region by the respective cluster C parameters. Sub-step tuning at different SOC levels is performed similarly to step 1, where 50% SOC level is tuned first, and 100 and 20% levels are followed.

step 4: This last step is designed to further tune the Warburg region with parameters showcasing Warburg region-specific sensitivities. Diffusion coefficients (cluster D) are tuned at the three SOC levels to capture their SOC-dependent variations.

ce:section.ce:para: Considering the tuned parameters, most are CT-sensitive (13 out of 15); therefore, the parameterisation is initiated with the CT tuning step. The first step of the parameterisation framework is designed to provide basis-tuned values for the CT-sensitive parameter clusters. Although cluster C is sensitive to both CT and Warburg, it is included in the CT-aimed first step considering the influence of the cluster on the CT region. The second step is purposed to provide inter-step cross-validation within the framework. Cluster C is sensitive to the full Nyquist spectra, i.e. both CT and Warburg, and already tuned with the CT data in the first step. The second step analyses the model fitting for the full Nyquist spectra, where the Warburg region can be treated as a cross-validation data set with different dynamics to the CT region. Although this inter-sensitivity-region cross-validation strategy differs from the general model validation concept used in data science, it can still be treated as a cross-validation technique due to its model performance cross-comparison between two distinct data domains. Step 3 repeats the optimisation strategy followed in step 1 whilst cluster C parameterisation boundaries are narrowed to 0.8–1.2 times the results found in step 2 for cluster C parameters. These narrow boundaries are included to avoid considerable changes in the Warburg region by the respective cluster C parameters. Finally, step 4 fits diffusion-related parameters (cluster D) using Warburg impedance data as target values.

The parameterisation framework described in this section was developed in the Matlab R2022b software package and linked to the COMSOL P2D cell model for model simulations via the Matlab Livelink bridge.

The core of each parameterisation step included in the parameterisation framework is the PSO-based metaheuristic approach, which attempts to find a minimal to the cost function derived from experimental and model-simulated impedance data. This metaheuristic approach is described in the following section.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Particle swarm optimisation with Darwinian events
ce:section.ce:para: Particle swarm optimisation (PSO) is a metaheuristic search algorithm that benefits from the evolutionary advantage offered by the social sharing of information among consociates, a theory inspired by the natural phenomena of fish schooling or bird flocking [81]. In PSO, a group of particles, each representing a potential solution to an optimisation problem, are iteratively updated based on the collective experience of the swarm [81]. Additionally, in this work, we fused Darwinian events into PSO, which incorporates ideas from the theory of evolution and benchmark analysis results showed capable of improving the performance of the algorithm.

Each particle ( i ) keeps track of its best position in the solution space (personal best,
p
i
k
), which yielded the optimal cost function value up to the current iteration ( k ). Then, the personal bests among the swarm of the particles are considered, and the best value is picked as the global best (
g
i
k
), representing the tentative best solution identified for the optimisation problem so far by the PSO. At each iteration of PSO, each particle’s position is updated with velocities ( v ), generating a new position. Considering an arbitrary particle of i , the new position x of the particle for
k + 1

w is the inertia term, which decides the dependency on the particle’s previous iteration direction.
c
1
and
c
2
are self-adjustment and social-adjustment weights which can scale the cognitive and social components, respectively. The cognitive and social components determine how much the particle’s personal and global best positions affect the particle’s evolvement in the solution space. Larger
c
1
values enhance the exploration by favouring particles evolving close to their personal best, while higher
c
2
values strengthen exploitation nature by driving particles to the global best.

In this study, Matlab’s ‘particleswarm’ function [82] was used to achieve PSO, and initial particle positions for PSO were generated using the Sobol quasi-random points generator function ‘sobolset’ [83].

ce:list-item.ce:para:
Cost function The cost function (CF) used in PSO is the mean-square-error (MSE) between the model-simulated impedance,

ce:list-item.ce:label: II.
ce:list-item.ce:para:
Darwinian events In this study, we fused Darwinian events into the general Matlab PSO function to improve the optimisation accuracy and robustness while maintaining computational times within reasonable limits. Incorporating Darwinian events, which introduces more randomness to the metaheuristic approach, showed better performance over the general PSO functionality with the same swarm size. The PSO procedure used in this study with 5 consequent phases can be summarised as follows:
i.
Initialisation phase with cross-reproduction The PSO process is initiated with this phase, and randomly generated particles with Sobol sequencing are introduced as initial values. During the Sobol sequence generation, random values between 0.1 and 0.9 of parameter ranges are used to avoid particles initiating with the parameter boundary values. This phase continues up to 4 iterations and uses comparatively higher
c
1
weights to enforce better initial solution space exploration. The selected PSO-related hyperparameters with trial-and-error procedures for best algorithm performance are given in Table S1 (supplementary information). At the end of each phase, PSO solutions yielding realistic impedance spectra are selected and passed to subsequent optimisation steps. A realistic result is defined as one where the peak imaginary impedance of the negative electrode CT semi-circle occurs at a higher frequency than that of the positive electrode. This particle selection criteria align with the electrode level EIS observed by Chen et al. for the LGM50 cell in [22]. Similarly, CT semi-circle peak impedance observed at higher frequencies for the negative electrode compared to the positive electrode is reported for other cell chemistries [18,84–86]. Then, these selected particles are used as parents to generate new particles to fill the number of penalised particles. This evolvement of particles by cross-combination of more suitable particles resembles a Darwinian event.
ii.
Cognitive exploration phase (with locked and unlocked social influence settings) This stage of PSO receives particles capable of generating more realistic EIS spectra from the previous phase and allows the particles to further explore the solution space in two settings. With suitably selected PSO hyperparameters, first, particles are encouraged to do cognitive exploration for 3 iterations with no social influence (zero
c
2
setting). Then, in the second setting, some social influence is unlocked for four further iterations. At the end of this phase, similar to the initialisation phase, realistic simulations are selected (amongst the swarm) referring to electrode level CT peak frequencies and new particles are generated by the cross-combination method (Darwinian event) as described in the initialisation phase.
iii.
Collaborative exploration phase with slow convergence After 11 total iterations, further collaborative exploration is allowed for 30 more iterations with higher PSO hyperparameters settings to continue the exploration. Then, assuming a reliable global exploration is achieved after 41 iterations, all the hyperparameter values are lowered for slow convergence to the global best.

ce:section.ce:para: This proposing parameter identification technique of multi-step, multi-SOC framework combined with PSO algorithm fused with Darwinian events is termed as MMPSOd in this paper.

A commercially available high-energy cylindrical LG M50 21700 (LGM50) lithium-ion battery comprising an NMC 811 positive electrode and bi-component Graphite-SiOx negative electrode was studied. The battery has a nominal voltage of 3.63 V and a nominal energy capacity of 18.20 Wh. The typical operating range of the battery is from 2.5 V to 4.2 V. Fig. S1 (supplementary information) illustrates the experimental setup, and the experimental procedure to generate the dataset for the data-driven parameterisation of the P2D model parameters is summarised in Table S2. Initial cell conditioning was carried out first by C/24 charge and discharge steps, followed by constant current charge–discharge and EIS data collection steps. Experimental measurements were carried out using an ARBIN battery cycler (LBT21084HC) with MITS Pro 8.0 software interface, and the battery cycler was connected with a GAMRY 1010E galvanostat/potentiostat for EIS measurements. Galvanostatic EIS was performed on the cell at three different SOC levels, i.e. 100%, 50% and 20%, using a sweeping current signal of 100 mA of amplitude and frequency ranging from 400 Hz to 4 mHz with 10 points per frequency decade. All the cell tests were performed in an ARBIN multi-zone temperature chamber (MZTC) maintained at 25 ± 0.5 °C.

This section presents the validations of the proposing data-driven model parameterisation technique with a virtual cell and experimental data from a commercially available cell. Virtual cell data with known parameter values are utilised to benchmark the multi-step, multi-SOC framework and Darwinian events fused PSO metaheuristic algorithm (MMPSOd) against generic PSO (no Darwinian events) fused with the parameterisation framework (MMPSOg) and generic PSO with single-step optimisation (PSOg). Numerical validation with the virtual cell data helps understand the advantages of the proposing MMPSOd for accurate parameter identification compared to the benchmark techniques. Finally, the model performance with parameters identified with the proposed data-driven non-invasive framework is presented and compared with the model performance with experimentally identified parameters to compare the parameter identification abilities of MMPSOd with state-of-the-art experimental measurements.

Considering that even the experimental invasive measurements cannot guarantee 100% accurate cell parameter measurements, the proposing model parameterisation framework was first validated numerically with a virtual cell. The same P2D model described in Section 2.1 was used as the virtual cell model and assigned randomly determined parameter values with the parameter boundaries summarised in Table 2. Then, the virtual cell model was simulated to generate the EIS target data for the data-driven parameterisation framework and other benchmark algorithms.

The performances of two parameter identification variations were considered benchmarks for assessing the performance with the proposing multi-step, multi-SOC parameterisation framework with Darwinian events fused PSO (MMPSOd). The first variation was the generic PSO (PSOg) approach, where PSO is used in its standalone format for parameter identification, i.e. parameters are optimised in a single optimisation run and use the generic version of PSO in its original form. This more generic approach of model parameterisation resembles the data-driven parameterisation routes followed in most of the literature. The second variation for performance comparison was the multi-step, multi-SOC parameterisation framework with the generic PSO (MMPSOg), which does not include the Darwinian events in the PSO algorithm.

Parameter identification performance with 100% SOC virtual cell data was assessed to deduce the performance matrices. The resulting EIS model fitting results are illustrated in Fig. 5(a), where MMPSOd shows better model fitting with the virtual cell target data than the other two benchmarks. Absolute percentage errors (APEs) between the virtual cell parameter values and algorithm-predicted parameter values are reported in Table S3 and, Fig. 5(b) depicts the APEs of the each identified parameters averaged over 5 runs to negate the effect of the randomness of the metaheuristic approach. It can be clearly seen that most of the parameters have been identified better by the MMPSOg compared to PSOg; this is due to the multi-step, multi-SOC approach, which utilises the parameter sensitivities and employs the best parameter identification impedance regions for each parameter cluster. MMPSOg has identified the negative electrode active specific area factor
a
f
v −
and reaction rate coefficients k with considerably better accuracy than PSOg, where these parameters showed the highest sensitivities among the 15 identified parameters. MMPSOd has performed better in identifying many parameters with lower APE values than MMPSOg, where the two techniques differ by the additional randomness in the MMPSOd, proving effective in avoiding local minima in the highly non-linear optimisation problem.

Comparatively higher APEs observed in diffusivity-related parameters in Fig. 5(b) are due to the larger parameter ranges spread across multiple magnitudes (Table 2) used for these parameters in the optimisation. Therefore, logarithmic scale-based APE calculations are better suitable for such parameters; nevertheless, a linear scale was used to maintain consistency across APE calculations for all parameters. On the other hand, higher parameter ranges, stemming from larger uncertainties in the estimations based on experimental measurement procedures on diffusivity-related parameters [63,64,87], cause more challenges for the optimisation algorithm; hence more deviations from the actual values are expected.

Table 5 summarises the mean APE (MAPE) performance for all the tuned parameters (
M A P
E
t o t
) and, impedance root-mean-square-error (
R M S
E
z
) between virtual cell EIS and EIS simulations with three identified parameter sets. Multi-step parameterisation approach has a clear effectiveness of the parameter identification, where MMPSOg has reduced the MAPE values of the total parameters by 33% compared to PSOg. This is further improved with MMPSOd with a reduction of the
M A P
E
t o t
by 52% compared to the generic PSO algorithm predictions.

Computationally, the PSOg algorithm offered about 5 times faster optimisation times due to its simpler structure compared to the MMPSOd algorithm. Whereas MMPSOd took about 1.5 times longer computational time than the MMPSOg due to the additional data transfer steps between COMSOL and Matlab platforms triggered by the Darwinian events included in MMPSOd.

With numerical benchmarks indicating better capabilities of the proposing MMPSOd methodology in parameter identification of the P2D model, we continued employing MMPSOd in parameter identification of the LGM50 cell, utilising experimental EIS data. The mechanism behind MMPSOd is the framework described in Section 2.3.3, which employs multi-step parameter optimisation with EIS data measured at three SOC levels, 100%, 50% and 20%, as depicted in Fig. 4 flowchart. EIS data for the data-driven parameter identification were collected at the three SOC levels following the experimental procedure reported in Table S2. MMPSOd was initiated with 150 particles, while PSO-hyperparameters were set at the pre-determined trial and error values reported in Table S1. The ensemble simulation framework was simulated with an Intel Xeon 6248 dual-processor setup with 40 cores with a base clock of 2.5 GHz. While a single EIS simulation with the COMSOL P2D model took around 20 s, the complete impedance-driven P2D parameter identification took approximately 7 days to complete. As most of the total computational time was spent on data transferring through the Matlab Livelink communication bridge (between Matlab and COMSOL), this computational time can be significantly reduced in several folds by implementing the P2D model in the Matlab environment directly, which is planned as future work.

Fig. 6 illustrates the EIS fitting results of the P2D model at 100%, 50% and 20% SOC levels to experimental data. It can be seen that MMPSOd-based data-driven parameter identification has achieved EIS fittings with good agreement with the experimental target EIS. Whilst RMSE values are below 1
m Ω
limit for 100%, and 20% SOCs, RMSE around 2
m Ω
is reported for the 50% SOC simulation. This higher error between experimental and simulated EIS spectra at 50% SOC may be due to the parameter compensation to a significant reduction of CT resistance caused by increased exchange current densities at mid-SOC levels [22,23]. A carefully chosen individual tuning-weight values for CT and Warburg regions in the cost function described in Eq. (4) can be used further to reduce the total error at these SOC levels.

The EIS impedance data-fitted P2D model was further analysed for the model performance with simulating voltage profiles of different dynamics. Fig. 7(a) depicts the experimental voltage validation results of the model using a 0.2C constant current (CC) discharge followed by a 4 h voltage relaxation representing slow discharge of the cell with idling, Fig. 7(b) shows 1C discharge pulse with voltage relaxation mimicking sudden load requirements and, Fig. 7(c) illustrates Worldwide Harmonized Light-Duty Vehicles Test Procedure (WLTP) high-dynamic duty cycle representing real-world operating conditions of the cell. According to authors knowledge, this is the first time an EIS-only fitted P2D model has been validated with transient duty cycles in the time domain to assess the feasibility of an impedance-based parameter identification technique. It is clear from Fig. 7 that proposing MMPSOd parameterisation algorithm has performed significantly well with voltage profiles with different dynamics. A relatively higher RMSE of 159 mV was seen for the voltage relaxation segment of the 0.2C assessment, which can be due to relatively poor fitting of the Warburg-sensitive parameters and, model inaccuracies in representing the real system.

Table 6 compares the parameters identified with the invasive experimental techniques [22,23] and, MMPSOd identified parameters with EIS model fitting. The MMPSOd identified parameter set falls within the same order of magnitudes as the experimentally measured parameter set while maintaining the physical soundness of the parameters. It is worth noting that the negative electrode particle radius (
r
p
−
) shows an interesting trend with the SOC values, where the radius decreases as the cell is further discharged, which could be correlated with the shrinkage of the particles with the delithiation process [22]. Active material solid phase diffusivities (
D
s
+
and
D
s
−
) also follow the general trend of diffusivity variation with the electrode stoichiometry as experimentally shown by O’regan et al. [23]. On the other hand, as experimental measurements are suffered from measurement errors, the differences between the two parameter sets, i.e. experimentally measured and MMPSOd fitted, cannot be considered a good metric to evaluate the performance of the MMPSOd technique.

The model’s performance was further compared utilising MMPSOd-identified parameters and experimentally identified cell parameters (on the same model of cell but from a different batch) following invasive laboratory techniques, including cell opening by Chen et al. [22] and O’regan et al. [23]. Fig. 8 compares the model’s EIS and voltage profile simulations utilising the two parameter identification techniques with the laboratory-measured cell outputs. The improvement in simulation accuracy with impedance-driven parameter identification is visible across all the tested EIS and voltage profiles. EIS is compared at 100% SOC in Fig. 8(a) and MMPSOd has fitted the EIS data significantly well compared to the model simulations with experimentally identified parameters, as expected. Not only has data-driven parameter identification shown a 28% reduction of the RMSE of 0.2C discharge case compared to the benchmark case (Table 7), but also the error histogram in Fig. S5(a) shows a narrower spread. Considering Fig. 8(c), the performance differences are more distinct with the pulse tests, where MMPSOd showed significantly better model fitting performance with an RMSE of 5 mV (Table 7), nearly 18 times error reduction over the experimental model fit error. Considering the reproducibility of automotive duty cycles, referring Fig. 8(d), the proposing MMPSOd parameter identification has better simulated the WLTP profile. This is evident in Table 7 with 65% less simulation error than the counterpart parameters set. The improvements in the error histogram (Fig. S2(c)) with MMPSOd, which is more centred on the zero error with narrower spreads, indicate the better suitability of the impedance-based data-driven parameter identification over invasive and time-consuming experimental parameterisation technique. The relatively higher errors observed with the voltage profile simulations using experimentally identified parameters are due to higher polarisations induced by the particular data set. As suggested by the MMPSOd data-fitted parameters set in Table 6, one possible cause of higher activation polarisation could be due to lower kinetic reaction rate coefficients, which are susceptible to higher estimation errors during measurement and estimation procedure followed, which includes ECM fitting to EIS data.

Regarding the comparatively larger errors observed for experimentally measured parameter set, in [22], authors chose to tune some of the most sensitive parameters to voltage profiles (including solid-phase diffusion coefficients, Bruggeman constants for both electrodes and separator and electrode maximum lithium concentrations) in order to achieve better fittings with the validation tests. During the tuning process, some tuned parameters have reported larger variations from the initially measured values (such as negative electrode diffusion coefficient, which was reported to have a 1797% increment after tuning for 1C discharge and relaxation test). It is worth noting that these tuned parameter values from [22] were not considered when deriving the comparisons illustrated in Fig. 8 and the results summarised in Tables 6 and 7; instead, the initial experimentally measured parameter values are utilised to highlight the differences between the model validations using the MMPSOd and experimentally measured parameter sets. On the other hand, in general, higher errors observed with the experimentally identified parameters compared to the MMPSOd technique may be partly due to tested LGM50 cells coming from different batches with possible design changes and performance variations due to manufacturing tolerances.

This work highlights the possibility of utilising the EIS data for successful parameter identification by incorporating a multi-step, multi-SOC parameterisation framework. Although data-driven parameterisation indicates better performance than experimentally determined parameter sets, a hybrid technique combining experimental measurements and data-driven parameter identification may offer better overall model fitting, as measured parameters help reduce the complexity of the optimisation problem significantly for the parameter identification algorithm. Moreover, compared to other data types used in the data-driven parameter identification techniques, such as steady state charge–discharge voltage data and standard duty cycles, EIS require significantly shorter measurement times which can help with faster model tuning for online BMS operations, with the advent of accurate on-board EIS measurement advancements.

Despite the extensive utilisation of voltage-based multi-step optimisation schemes for P2D model parameterisation, such a multi-step parameter optimisation framework has not previously been applied to impedance-based analysis. Therefore, in this work, we proposed a multi-step, multi-SOC P2D model kinetic parameter identification framework methodically utilising impedance data with accurate real-world dynamic duty cycle simulation viabilities. Some highlights of the proposed parameter identification framework are mentioned below.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Parameter identification is performed only using the EIS impedance data. The framework identifies fifteen kinetic P2D model parameters using a systematic approach based on parameter sensitivity and inter-step cross-validation to avoid overfitting.

The multi-step approach properly utilises the parameter sensitivities to CT and Warburg Nyquist regions to improve the identifiability of each parameter cluster, increasing the accuracy of the parameter identification. Parameter identification with the multi-SOC sub-step technique allows the framework to identify SOC-dependent parameters, which increases the model accuracy across the full SOC range of the cell.

PSO fused with Darwinian events (MMPSOd) proved better performance with numerical validations compared to the generic version of PSO (PSOg), where MMPSOd was observed to have 25% lower RMSE than PSOg.

As an off-line parameter identification technique, the proposing EIS-based parameter identification framework offers several experimental advantages over other data-driven methods. Considerably faster measurement times of EIS compared to other measurements, such as testing at different C-rates, multi-pulse discharge profiles and highly-dynamic duty cycles, significantly reduce the experimental burden.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jpowsour.2023.233400.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary material related to this article.
MMC S1
This document provides additional information on the experimental setup and identified fitted model parameters.
